cot_prefix: Okay, let's look at the question and the provided text to find the correct answer. The question asks what the Indians believed according to the letter. The letter is a response from the Six Nations to an offer to send their boys to William and Mary College. The letter starts by acknowledging the officials' offer and their intention to do good. The Indians express gratitude but state that different nations have different ways of looking at things. They then explain that their young people who went to the northern colleges came back with bad manners and were unable to live in the woods, implying that the education didn't prepare them for their traditional way of life. The key part here is when they say, "we have had some experience of it. Several of our young people were formerly brought up at the colleges of the northern provinces: they were taught all your sciences; but when they came back to us, they were bad manners, ignorant of every means of living in the woods-they were totally good for nothing." This suggests that the education system the officials are offering isn't effective or suitable according to the Indians' perspective. Then, they offer an alternative: if the officials send their own sons, the Indians will teach them in their ways and make men of them. This shows they value their own methods of education. The options given are: (A) it would be better for their boys to receive some schooling (B) they were being insulted by the offer (C) they knew more about science than the officials (D) they had better way of educating young men Looking at the text, the Indians do not say they know more about science (C is incorrect).
